exercises.title: Sync vs async — which to use?
exercises.scenario: Some operations need an immediate response; others can proceed without one.
exercises.instructions:
For each operation, should the calling service use synchronous (REST) or asynchronous (event/queue) communication?

A. Order service checking if a product is in stock before confirming an order
B. Sending a welcome email after user registration
C. Charging a user's payment before order confirmation
D. Updating a recommendation model when a user views a product
E. Checking user's credit limit before approving a loan
F. Logging a completed transaction to an analytics warehouse
exercises.expected_solution:
A → Synchronous (need answer before proceeding)
B → Asynchronous (can proceed without waiting for email)
C → Synchronous (must confirm payment before confirming order)
D → Asynchronous (recommendation update can be eventual)
E → Synchronous (need credit limit answer to approve/reject)
F → Asynchronous (logging can be eventual, doesn't affect transaction)

test_cases.expected:
A → Synchronous (need answer before proceeding)
B → Asynchronous (can proceed without waiting for email)
C → Synchronous (must confirm payment before confirming order)
D → Asynchronous (recommendation update can be eventual)
E → Synchronous (need credit limit answer to approve/reject)
F → Asynchronous (logging can be eventual, doesn't affect transaction)

hints: Synchronous: you need the answer to proceed with the current operation
Asynchronous: the current operation can complete without waiting

exercises.title: RestTemplate — GET and POST to another service
exercises.scenario: RestTemplate makes HTTP calls to other Spring Boot services.

blanks.hint: GET request that deserializes the response body
POST request with request body, deserializes response

hints: getForObject: GET, deserialize body to object
postForObject: POST with body, deserialize response

exercises.title: RestTemplate @Bean configuration
exercises.scenario: RestTemplate must be declared as a Spring bean.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete `AppConfig` @Configuration class that:
1. Declares a `RestTemplate` @Bean
2. Shows how to inject it into a @Service via constructor
3. Shows one example call to another service
hints: @Bean RestTemplate: Spring manages the instance
Inject via constructor: OrderService(RestTemplate rt)
restTemplate.getForObject(url, Type.class): GET and deserialize

exercises.title: Decompose a monolith into services
exercises.scenario: Identify service boundaries from a monolith.
exercises.instructions:
Given this monolith package structure for a Zerodha-style trading app, propose 4 microservices with their responsibilities and communication patterns:

```
com.zerodha.trading/
  user/       - login, profile, KYC
  portfolio/  - holdings, P&L calculation
  orders/     - buy/sell orders, order book
  market/     - stock prices, market data
  payments/   - fund transfers, withdrawals
  alerts/     - price alerts, notifications
```
hints: Group by business domain (user, market, order, payment)
Sync if you need the answer to proceed; async if not
Each service owns its own data

exercises.title: RestTemplate GET with ResponseEntity
exercises.scenario: ResponseEntity gives access to both status code and body.
exercises.instructions:
Write `PriceService.getStockPrice(String symbol)` that:
1. Calls market-service: GET /api/market/price/{symbol}
2. Uses getForEntity() to get the ResponseEntity<PriceResponse>
3. If 200: returns the price
4. If 404 (symbol not found): throws SymbolNotFoundException
5. If other error: throws MarketServiceException
hints: getForEntity(): returns ResponseEntity<T> with status + body
HttpClientErrorException.NotFound: thrown by RestTemplate on 404
RestClientException: parent of all RestTemplate errors

exercises.title: Minimal product-service — standalone Spring Boot
exercises.scenario: Each microservice is a complete Spring Boot application.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete minimal `product-service` Spring Boot application:
1. `ProductServiceApplication` with main()
2. `Product` @Entity (id, name, price, stockQty)
3. `ProductRepository`
4. `ProductController` with GET /api/products and GET /api/products/{id}
5. application.properties: server.port=8081, H2 datasource

This runs independently on port 8081.
exercises.starter_code:
// product-service — complete standalone Spring Boot app
// server.port=8081
hints: Separate @SpringBootApplication: this is its own deployable unit
server.port=8081: product-service runs on 8081
spring.application.name=product-service: used by Eureka in Topic 15.3

exercises.title: order-service calling product-service
exercises.scenario: order-service makes a sync HTTP call to product-service before placing an order.
exercises.instructions:
Write `OrderService.placeOrder(String productId, int quantity)` that:
1. Calls product-service GET /api/products/{productId} via RestTemplate
2. If product not found (null response): throws ProductNotFoundException
3. If quantity > product.stockQty: throws InsufficientStockException
4. Otherwise: saves and returns the order
5. Hardcode product-service URL as 'http://localhost:8081'
hints: HttpClientErrorException.NotFound: RestTemplate throws this on 404
product == null check: getForObject returns null on 204 No Content
Hardcode URL for now — Eureka service discovery replaces this in T15.3

exercises.title: Mock downstream services in @SpringBootTest
exercises.scenario: Use MockRestServiceServer to test without real downstream services.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test for `OrderService.placeOrder()` that:
1. Uses MockRestServiceServer to mock the product-service HTTP call
2. Test case 1: product found, stock sufficient → order placed
3. Test case 2: product found, stock insufficient → InsufficientStockException
4. Test case 3: product not found (404) → ProductNotFoundException
hints: MockRestServiceServer: intercepts RestTemplate calls — no real HTTP
withSuccess(body, contentType): mock 200 response with JSON body
withStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND): mock 404 response

exercises.title: Inter-service DTO design — what to expose
exercises.scenario: Services expose only what other services need, not their full domain model.
exercises.instructions:
Design the inter-service DTOs for order-service calling user-service:
1. `UserResponse` — what order-service needs from user-service (not full user, just enough)
2. `OrderEventDto` — what order-service publishes after an order (for notification-service)
3. Write the calling code in OrderService using these DTOs
hints: UserResponse: only the fields order-service needs — not password, not all preferences
OrderEventDto: enough for notification-service to send a useful SMS/email
Principle: expose minimal, stable interface — don't share internal domain objects

exercises.title: Data isolation — what's wrong here?
exercises.expected_solution:
Problem 1: Both order-service and (presumably) product-service share the same database URL (company_db). This violates data isolation.
Problem 2: order-service has a ProductRepository that directly queries the products table — this belongs to product-service.
Problem 3: order-service is coupled to product-service's database schema. Any schema change in product-service breaks order-service.

Correct approach:
- order-service uses orders_db (its own database)
- product-service uses products_db (its own database)
- order-service gets product data by calling product-service REST API
- No direct cross-service database access

test_cases.expected:
Problem 1: Both order-service and (presumably) product-service share the same database URL (company_db). This violates data isolation.
Problem 2: order-service has a ProductRepository that directly queries the products table — this belongs to product-service.
Problem 3: order-service is coupled to product-service's database schema. Any schema change in product-service breaks order-service.

Correct approach:
- order-service uses orders_db (its own database)
- product-service uses products_db (its own database)
- order-service gets product data by calling product-service REST API
- No direct cross-service database access

hints: Shared database: the #1 microservices antipattern
Each service must own its own schema
Cross-service data access: only via REST API, never direct DB

exercises.title: RestTemplate POST and DELETE inter-service
exercises.scenario: Full CRUD inter-service calls for a reservation system.
hints: HttpClientErrorException.Conflict: 409 response — seat already reserved
rt.delete(url): DELETE with no response body
Catch specific exceptions before catching the general RestClientException

exercises.title: Exchange — POST with custom headers
exercises.scenario: exchange() handles any HTTP method with full control over headers and body.
exercises.instructions:
Write `PaymentService.processPayment()` that calls payment-gateway-service with:
1. POST /api/payments with JSON body
2. Custom headers: X-API-Key (from @ConfigurationProperties), X-Idempotency-Key (UUID)
3. Handles 200 (success), 402 (payment required/declined), 429 (rate limited)
4. Returns PaymentResult
hints: exchange(url, method, HttpEntity<body>, ResponseType.class)
HttpEntity<T>(body, headers): combines body + headers for exchange()
HttpStatus.PAYMENT_REQUIRED = 402; TOO_MANY_REQUESTS = 429

exercises.title: Two-service integration — order places call to inventory
exercises.scenario: End-to-end: order-service calls inventory-service, mock server in test.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete two-service setup with tests:
1. `inventory-service`: InventoryItem (productId, available, stockQty), GET /api/inventory/{productId}
2. `order-service`: OrderService calls inventory-service before placing order
3. `@SpringBootTest` test with `MockRestServiceServer` for all three scenarios: success, out of stock, service down

All 3 tests must pass.
exercises.starter_code:
// Complete two-service integration test
// InventoryItem record, OrderService calling inventory, MockRestServiceServer tests
hints: MockRestServiceServer.createServer(restTemplate): intercepts calls from that RestTemplate
withException(new IOException(...)): simulates connection failure
ResourceAccessException: RestTemplate throws this on connection errors

exercises.title: Propagate authentication token between services
exercises.scenario: JWT from the client must be forwarded to downstream services.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `TokenPropagationInterceptor` that:
1. Implements `ClientHttpRequestInterceptor`
2. Reads the JWT from the current SecurityContext
3. Adds it as Authorization: Bearer {token} header on outgoing RestTemplate calls
4. Register it on the RestTemplate @Bean

This ensures inter-service calls carry the user's JWT.
hints: ClientHttpRequestInterceptor: intercepts every RestTemplate request
SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().getCredentials(): the JWT string
request.getHeaders().set(AUTHORIZATION, 'Bearer '+token): add header to outgoing call

exercises.title: Complete Swiggy-style order flow — Topic 15.1 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Full inter-service orchestration for a food delivery order.
exercises.instructions:
Build an order orchestration service (Swiggy-style):
- `OrderOrchestrationService.placeOrder(userId, restaurantId, items)` must:
  1. Call user-service: GET /api/users/{id}/status → active check (sync)
  2. Call restaurant-service: GET /api/restaurants/{id}/available → open check (sync)
  3. Calculate total from items
  4. Call payment-service: POST /api/payments → charge (sync)
  5. Save order
  6. (Conceptual) publish 'order.placed' for delivery and notification (async, print only)

- Use MockRestServiceServer in @SpringBootTest for all 3 HTTP calls
- Test: success, user inactive (reject), restaurant closed (reject), payment failed (reject)
- All 4 tests pass
exercises.starter_code:
// Complete Swiggy-style order orchestration
// 3 downstream HTTP calls + MockRestServiceServer tests
hints: 3 separate server.expect() calls — they fire in order
MockRestServiceServer: each expect matches the next HTTP call made
Helper methods (mockUser, mockRestaurant, mockPayment) reduce duplication
